file_name: Mehdar et al. - 2026 - Deep neural network-based robust framework for automated skin lesion segmentation and analysis.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a deep neural network (DNN)-based framework for automated skin lesion segmentation from dermoscopic images. The method integrates a DNN architecture with pre-processing and post-processing modules to extract hierarchical features and refine segmentation masks. It was tested on ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2018 datasets, achieving Jaccard index and Dice coefficient scores that outperformed state-of-the-art methods like U-Net, UNet++, and Swin-Unet.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'Original research article' in the header. It presents a novel deep neural network-based framework for skin lesion segmentation, detailing specific methods (pre-processing, DNN architecture, post-processing), experimental validation on ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2018 datasets, and original quantitative results (Jaccard index, Dice coefficient) compared against baseline models.

title: Deep neural network-based robust framework for automated skin lesion segmentation and analysis
brief_summary: This study developed a deep neural network-based framework for automated skin lesion segmentation to address challenges in early skin cancer diagnosis. The method integrates specialized pre-processing for noise reduction and intensity normalization with a DNN for hierarchical feature extraction and post-processing for boundary refinement. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2018 datasets without extensive data augmentation. It achieved Jaccard index scores of 89.91%, 84.51%, and 87.39%, and Dice coefficients of 94.30%, 90.86%, and 92.53% respectively. These results outperformed state-of-the-art methods such as U-Net, UNet++, and Swin-Unet in accuracy and computational efficiency. The findings support the framework's robustness and suitability for real-world clinical applications in computer-aided diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Binary mask delineating skin lesion regions from dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a deep neural network-based framework for automated skin lesion segmentation. The model predicts binary masks (predicted vs ground truth) to delineate lesion boundaries. Performance is evaluated using segmentation metrics: Jaccard Index (JI), Dice Coefficient (DC), Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Specificity. The task involves pixel-wise classification to separate lesion foreground from background, not predicting discrete diagnostic categories (classification) or continuous numeric outcomes (regression).

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Neural Network (DNN) framework with pre-processing and post-processing modules

architecture_details.description: A compact DNN designed for hierarchical feature extraction, integrated with classical image enhancement steps. The framework emphasizes simplicity, modularity, and adaptability over complex network architectures.
components: DNN segmentation model (multi-scale hierarchical feature extraction)

training_details.loss_function: Combined Binary Cross-Entropy and Dice Loss
training_details.training_schedule: Trained on ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2018 datasets; hyperparameters finalized based on validation results and kept consistent across datasets

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Probability map indicating likelihood of each pixel belonging to the lesion class
post_processing_steps: Removal of small disconnected components below a fixed area threshold

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported, but described as 'significantly lower parameter count' compared to DAGAN and FAT-Net, and 'compact' compared to models requiring tens of millions of parameters.

preprocessing_techniques: Morphological operations (top-hat and bottom-hat transformations) for background intensity correction and noise suppression
Adaptive Wiener filtering for noise reduction
Contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) applied optionally based on global intensity variance
Anisotropic diffusion filtering for edge enhancement and background smoothing
conclusions: The proposed deep neural network-based framework effectively addresses challenges in skin lesion segmentation by integrating robust feature extraction and boundary refinement, making it well-suited for real-world clinical applications in skin cancer diagnosis and management.

The method achieved high segmentation accuracy and consistency, outperforming state-of-the-art methods such as U-Net, UNet++, and Swin-Unet in terms of segmentation accuracy, consistency, and computational efficiency.

The full integration of pre-processing, segmentation, and post-processing stages leads to superior performance, with each module adding incremental value, particularly in diverse real-world conditions.

The proposed framework demonstrates computational efficiency and scalability, with an average processing time of 2.39 seconds per image, making it suitable for deployment in resource-constrained or real-time clinical applications.

The model showed strong generalization capabilities, maintaining high performance on external validation datasets (PH2 and HAM10000) without retraining, indicating robustness under real-world conditions with previously unseen clinical images.

Statistical analysis confirmed that the performance improvements over baseline models were statistically significant (p < 0.05), with notable gains in Dice coefficient (6.8% to 8.3%) and Jaccard index (8.8% to 11.1%) across ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2018 datasets.
important_remarks: The study explicitly states it was conducted 'without extensive data augmentation,' which may limit the model's robustness to variations not present in the training data.

The model was trained solely on ISIC datasets and evaluated on external datasets (PH2 and HAM10000) without any retraining, fine-tuning, or parameter adjustment, serving as a strict test of generalization.

The authors acknowledge limitations regarding lesions with 'extremely subtle boundaries or very low contrast against the skin background' and potential performance variability across rare visual patterns or specific lesion subtypes not represented in training.

The PH2 dataset used for external validation consists of images acquired under controlled conditions at a single clinical site, which may not fully represent the heterogeneity of real-world clinical environments.

The HAM10000 dataset evaluation highlights the model's ability to handle heterogeneity from multiple clinical environments and varying devices, but performance dropped slightly compared to ISIC datasets (e.g., Dice 0.86 vs 0.94 on ISIC 2016), indicating a generalization gap.

The framework prioritizes computational efficiency and simplicity over complex architectures, resulting in significantly fewer parameters than state-of-the-art methods, which is noted as a strength for resource-constrained deployment but may come at the cost of capturing highly complex features.

Statistical significance was confirmed using paired t-tests (after Shapiro-Wilk normality check) and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, with p < 0.05, supporting the reliability of the reported performance gains over baselines.
